title: Kuro and GCD and XOR and SUM
description: Initially there is an empty array a. The game consists of q tasks of two types. Type 1 asks to add a number u to a. Type 2 asks to find a number v in a such that k | GCD(x, v), x + v <= s, and x XOR v is maximized, where XOR denotes the bitwise XOR operation, or report -1 if no such number exists.
input_spec: The first line contains one integer q (2 <= q <= 10^5). q lines follow, each beginning with an integer t -- the type of the task. If t = 1, an integer u follows (1 <= u <= 10^5) -- add u to array a. If t = 2, three integers x, k, and s follow (1 <= x, k, s <= 10^5) -- find v in a such that k | GCD(x, v), x + v <= s, and x XOR v is maximized, or report -1.
output_spec: For each task of type 2, output on one line the desired number v, or -1 if no such numbers are found.

routes.name: Divisor-indexed tries with greedy XOR maximization
routes.description: Build one binary trie per divisor value. For each query, reduce the GCD constraint to a divisibility check, then greedily traverse the appropriate trie to maximize XOR while respecting the sum constraint.

observations.title: GCD constraint reduces to divisibility by k
observations.description: The condition k | GCD(x, v) holds if and only if both x and v are divisible by k. So if k does not divide x, the answer is immediately -1. Otherwise, we only need to consider values v in the array that are multiples of k.

hints.nudge: What does it mean for k to divide GCD(x, v)? What must be true about x and v individually?
hints.question: If GCD(x, v) is divisible by k, does that mean x is divisible by k? Does v have to be divisible by k?
hints.formal: k | GCD(x, v) iff k | x and k | v. This is because GCD(x,v) divides both x and v, and k divides GCD(x,v). Conversely, if k | x and k | v then k | GCD(x,v). So immediately return -1 if k does not divide x.

observations.title: One trie per divisor to handle the divisibility filter
observations.description: Maintain a separate binary trie for each possible divisor d (1 <= d <= MAX). Trie[d] contains all values in the array that are multiples of d. When a number u is added, insert it into trie[d] for every divisor d of u. For a query with divisor k, search only trie[k].

hints.nudge: You need fast access to all multiples of k in the array. How could you preorganize numbers by their divisors?
hints.question: If you insert a number u into a trie for each of its divisors d, how many tries does u go into? What is the total cost over all insertions?
hints.formal: When adding u, enumerate all divisors of u in O(sqrt(u)) and insert u into trie[d] for each divisor d. Total insertions across all adds: sum of d(u) for all u, bounded by O(MAX * log(MAX)) across all distinct values up to MAX = 10^5.

observations.title: Greedy bit-by-bit XOR maximization on a trie
observations.description: To maximize x XOR v, traverse the binary trie from the most significant bit to the least. At each bit, prefer the branch that differs from the corresponding bit of x (since differing bits produce 1 in the XOR). This is the classic trie-based XOR maximization.

hints.nudge: In XOR, you want as many high-order bits to be 1 as possible. At each bit, which branch of the trie gives a 1 in the XOR?
hints.question: If the current bit of x is b, going to the trie branch (1-b) gives a 1 in that bit of the XOR. When should you go to branch b instead?
hints.formal: At bit i, let b = (x >> i) & 1. Try to descend into child (b ^ 1) for a 1-bit in the XOR. If that child does not exist or cannot satisfy the sum constraint, descend into child b instead. This greedy approach from MSB to LSB maximizes the XOR.

observations.title: Enforce the sum constraint via minimum tracking in trie nodes
observations.description: Each trie node stores the minimum value in its subtree. When traversing to maximize XOR, only descend into a branch if its minimum value plus x does not exceed s. This ensures the sum constraint x + v <= s is satisfied without filtering candidates afterwards.

hints.nudge: You need x + v <= s, which means v <= s - x. How can you quickly tell if a trie branch contains any value small enough?
hints.question: If each trie node stores the minimum value among all numbers in its subtree, how would you use that during the greedy traversal?
hints.formal: Each node maintains min_val = min of all values in its subtree. When deciding to descend into a child, check child.min_val + x <= s. If the preferred branch (for XOR) fails this check, try the other branch. If neither works, return -1.

observations.title: Deduplication: only insert each distinct value once
observations.description: Since the problem adds values to the array and values can repeat, use a boolean array to track which values have already been inserted. Skip re-insertion of duplicates to avoid unnecessary trie operations and keep the tries compact.

hints.nudge: What happens if the same number is added multiple times? Does it change the answer to any query?
hints.question: If v is already in the array and is added again, does the set of reachable XOR values change? Is there any benefit to inserting it twice?
hints.formal: Maintain chk[u] = true if u has been added. On type 1 query, if chk[u] is true, skip. Duplicates do not affect the existence or optimality of any v, since we only need to find one such v.

observations.title: Complexity: precomputing divisors and trie bit-width
observations.description: Precompute for each number 1..MAX the list of its divisors using a sieve-like approach in O(MAX log MAX). Each trie has depth log2(MAX) ~ 17 bits. Queries run in O(17) per trie traversal. Total complexity is O(MAX log MAX + q * sqrt(MAX) + q * 17).

hints.nudge: How do you enumerate all divisors of every number up to MAX efficiently?
hints.question: For each d from 1 to MAX, you can mark d as a divisor of d, 2d, 3d, ... How does this relate to the harmonic series?
hints.formal: Sieve divisors: for d in 1..MAX, for j in d, 2d, ..., MAX: add d to divisors[j]. Total work: sum_{d=1}^{MAX} MAX/d = O(MAX * H(MAX)) = O(MAX log MAX). Trie depth: ceil(log2(10^5)) = 17. Each query does O(17) trie descent.
